serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single serving of Chicken Chettinad is approximately 250 g, typically plated as a generous portion of bone-in or boneless spiced chicken pieces with thick masala gravy, served alongside steamed rice, parotta, or appam.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Chicken Chettinad exists in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for cooked chicken preparations with spices and coconut, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) entries for cooked chicken and spice blends. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central coconut and chicken fat composition data. All values represent a plausible composite for this dish type.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate for high-protein, low-carbohydrate spiced chicken curry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Chettinad. GI estimated at 25 (low) based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate spiced chicken curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish contains only 3.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly from spices and small amounts of onion; the high protein (15.5 g) and fat (10.2 g) content further attenuate glycemic response. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (25 × 3.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 2.19, rounded to 2.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (350 mg per 100 g; ~875 mg per 250 g serving) and intense spice load including black pepper
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Chicken Chettinad to occasional consumption, request reduced-salt preparation, and monitor total daily sodium intake, as a single serving can contribute approximately 38% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by major cardiovascular guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.5 g per 100 g) and low estimated GI (~25) with high protein supporting glycemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken Chettinad is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate load and high protein content; however, portion size should be monitored to manage overall caloric intake, and the dish should be paired with low-GI accompaniments such as cauliflower rice rather than white rice or parotta.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from coconut (~3.8 g per 100 g) and high sodium (350 mg per 100 g) offset partially by anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Chicken Chettinad in moderation; the saturated fat from coconut and the high sodium content are concerns, but the anti-inflammatory properties of turmeric, ginger, and garlic may offer partial benefit — preparation with reduced coconut and salt is advisable for heart-health-conscious individuals.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic) may benefit joint inflammation, but high chili content may be pro-inflammatory in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chicken Chettinad may be consumed by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who tolerate spicy food well, given the anti-inflammatory properties of turmeric and ginger; those with spice sensitivity should request a milder preparation with reduced chili and black pepper.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Chettinad is a flagship dish of the Chettinad region in Tamil Nadu, South India, and is widely regarded as one of the most aromatic and intensely spiced chicken curries in Indian cuisine. The dish is defined by its freshly prepared 'Chettinad Masala' — a complex blend of dry-roasted and ground spices that includes kalpasi (stone flower), marathi moggu (kapok buds), star anise, fennel seeds, black peppercorns, and dried red chilies. The chicken, typically bone-in pieces, is slow-simmered with shallots, ginger, garlic, curry leaves, and fresh coconut, producing a thick, deeply aromatic gravy with multi-layered heat and an earthy, resinous aroma. Nutritionally, Chicken Chettinad provides approximately 165 kcal per 100 g, with 15.5 g protein, 10.2 g fat (including ~3.8 g saturated fat from coconut), and only 3.5 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 25. The high protein content supports satiety and muscle maintenance, while the spice blend contributes anti-inflammatory compounds including curcumin (from turmeric), gingerols (from ginger), and allicin (from garlic). Sodium content is relatively high at 350 mg per 100 g, which is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is traditionally served with steamed rice, parotta, or appam at lunch or dinner, and is a centrepiece of Tamil Nadu festive and celebratory meals. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is suitable for halal diets.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Chicken Chettinad exists in any single approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked chicken and spice preparation data, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, and polyunsaturated fat values are estimated from component-level data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable high-protein, low-carbohydrate spiced chicken preparations. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.8 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.5 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.9 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked chicken and coconut fat composition; these values are not directly reported in ICMR-NIN for this specific dish. 2. cholesterol_mg is set to -1: cholesterol data for this specific Chettinad preparation is not reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry. 3. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry. 4. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible) based on the absence of hydrogenated fats in the traditional preparation. 5. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Chicken Chettinad has been published in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2024. 6. The input record contained three duplicate portion entries (all '1 serving 250g'); deduplicated to a single default serving of 250 g. 7. Vitamin A value (15.0 mcg) is expressed as mcg RAE in the output, consistent with USDA reporting conventions; the original input expressed this as mcg without specifying RAE — the value is plausible for this dish type. 8. Sodium at 350 mg per 100 g (~875 mg per 250 g serving) is a significant concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management.
